subject: ap-world-history
title: Islamic World & State Building
stimulus:
“It was by the grace of God, glory be to Him, that He came to the rescue of Islam, by reviving its last breath
and restoring in Egypt the unity of the Muslims [in the mid-thirteenth century]. He did this by sending the
Mamluks to the Muslims as guardian rulers and devoted defenders, who were imported as slaves from the
lands of the heathen Qipchaq Turks. The Turkish slaves embrace Islam with the determination of true
believers, while retaining their nomadic virtues.
The slave merchants bring them to Egypt in batch after batch. Then the rulers lodge them in the royal
chambers, and give them a careful upbringing, including the study of the Qur’an. Then they train them in the
use of the bow and the sword, in riding horses, and in fighting with the lance until they become tough and
seasoned soldiers. When the rulers are convinced that they are prepared to defend and die for them, they
increase their pay, lands, and incomes. Then they appoint them to high offices of state, and even sultans are
chosen from them who direct the affairs of the Muslims.”
Source: Ibn Khaldun, Arab scholar and historian writing about the Mamluk
dynasty of Egypt, late fourteenth century

parts.prompt: Identify ONE claim that the author makes about the Mamluks in the first paragraph.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE specific claim Ibn Khaldun makes about the Mamluks in the first paragraph.

required_elements: A claim about the religious/providential role of the Mamluks in saving Islam
alternatives.correct_example: The Mamluks were chosen by God to save Egypt.

required_elements: A claim about the Mamluks' protective/defensive role toward Islam
alternatives.correct_example: The Mamluks were guardian rulers and defended Islam.

wrong_examples: A vague reference to 'the Mamluks did things' without naming a specific claim.
Summarizing the entire passage instead of one claim from paragraph 1.
A claim drawn from the second paragraph (about their training) — wrong paragraph.
common_traps: Conflating the training/promotion content in paragraph 2 with claims in paragraph 1.
Offering a student opinion about the Mamluks instead of identifying Ibn Khaldun's claim.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE claim that the author makes about the Mamluks in the first paragraph.

sample_responses.response: The Mamluks were chosen by God to save Egypt.
sample_responses.commentary: Directly identifies Ibn Khaldun's providential framing — 'by the grace of God... He came to the rescue of Islam.'

sample_responses.response: The Mamluks were guardian rulers and defended Islam.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the author's claim that Mamluks functioned as 'guardian rulers and devoted defenders.'

sample_responses.response: Many Turkish enslaved persons converted to Islam and were true believers, even though they remained nomadic.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the author's claim about the Mamluks' religious conversion combined with retention of nomadic virtues.
Too vague — does not identify a specific claim from the first paragraph.

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE way the passage illustrates the political situation of the Islamic world in the period before
circa 1450.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE way the passage illustrates the Islamic world's political situation before 1450.

required_elements: Identification of disunity or fragmentation among Islamic states
alternatives.correct_example: The passage illustrates the lack of unity among Islamic states.

required_elements: Identification of a specific state-building practice (military, religious, administrative) described in the passage
alternatives.correct_example: The passage illustrates that Islamic rulers invested heavily in soldiers.

wrong_examples: Identification of a political practice that is not actually illustrated in the passage.
A response about post-1450 political developments.
common_traps: Describing the Mamluks' religious role rather than the broader political situation.
Conflating the Mamluks' specific case with the wider Islamic world's political situation.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE way the passage illustrates the political situation of the Islamic world in the period before circa 1450.

sample_responses.response: The passage illustrates the lack of unity among Islamic states.
sample_responses.commentary: The passage's reference to 'restoring in Egypt the unity of the Muslims' implies prior disunity — a fragmented Islamic political landscape.

sample_responses.response: The passage illustrates that Islamic rulers invested heavily in soldiers.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the political practice of heavy military investment (training, pay, lands) shown in paragraph 2.

sample_responses.response: The passage illustrates that Islamic rulers patronized religious learning.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies patronage of religious learning (Qur'an study) as a feature of the pre-1450 Islamic political situation.

sample_responses.response: The passage demonstrates how Islamic rulers engaged in a variety of activities that increased their power, such as appointing trusted officials to important positions, training a powerful military, and supporting religious teachings.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies multiple legitimating and power-consolidating activities (official appointment, military, religion) characteristic of pre-1450 Islamic rule.

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE way the passage could be used to illustrate differences in forms of coerced labor in the
period before circa 1750.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for explaining ONE way the passage illustrates differences between Mamluk enslavement and other forms of coerced labor before 1750.

required_elements: Contrast between Mamluk upward mobility and enslaved persons in the Americas
Explanation of how the Mamluk system differed in outcomes
alternatives.correct_example: The formerly enslaved Mamluks could rise to high political positions, such as sultan, unlike enslaved persons in the Americas.

required_elements: Contrast based on purpose of slavery (political/military vs. economic)
alternatives.correct_example: The ability of the Mamluks to achieve high status in Egypt differs from other forms of coerced labor. In the Americas, for example, the purpose of slavery was mostly economic, and enslaved persons largely worked on plantations.

wrong_examples: Describing Mamluk slavery without a comparison to another form of coerced labor.
A contrast that ignores the time frame (post-1750 comparisons).
Vague gestures at 'slavery varied' without a specific differentiating feature.
common_traps: Focusing on conditions in the Americas without tying back to what the passage illustrates.
Making a general comparison without naming a specific contrast (e.g., economic vs. military purpose).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining ONE way the passage could be used to illustrate differences in forms of coerced labor in the period before circa 1750.

sample_responses.response: The formerly enslaved Mamluks could rise to high political positions, such as sultan, unlike enslaved persons in the Americas.
sample_responses.commentary: Clear contrast: Mamluk enslavement permitted upward mobility to sultan — unlike chattel slavery in the Americas where such mobility was impossible.

sample_responses.response: The ability of the Mamluks to achieve high status in Egypt differs from other forms of coerced labor. In the Americas, for example, the purpose of slavery was mostly economic, and enslaved persons largely worked on plantations.
sample_responses.commentary: Contrasts the political/military purpose of Mamluk enslavement with the economic/plantation purpose of Atlantic slavery.

sample_responses.response: Because the Mamluks were male, they were afforded the opportunity to receive military training and political promotion unlike enslaved women in Islamic societies.
sample_responses.commentary: Contrasts male Mamluk opportunities (military, political promotion) with restrictions placed on enslaved women in Islamic societies.

sample_responses.response: Slavery was different in many places.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — no specific comparison or mechanism identified.
